1
Email · First Touch
Day 1 · Cold outreach · Firmographic trigger
✉️ Email
ZD
Zak Dalal
zak@lemlist.com
To: Camilla Papa <camilla@acolad.com>
sales academy

Hi Camilla,

I noticed Acolad brought in a new CRO — and she's been pretty public about building a proper sales academy from the ground up.

That kind of initiative sounds exciting from the top. From a SalesOps perspective, it usually means new reps to onboard, sequences to standardise, and reporting to rebuild — all at the same time.

The thing that tends to break quietly during that kind of push: tool adoption. Playbook gets built, reps get trained, and by month two half of them are doing their own thing.

Is that something you're managing right now, or is the rollout still early?

Saket (Zak) Dalal
Key Account Manager

Day 5 · New angle · Peer story
✉️ Email
ZD
Zak Dalal
zak@lemlist.com
To: Camilla Papa <camilla@acolad.com>
the playbook gap

Hi Camilla,

A SalesOps lead at a 200-person SaaS company said something to me last month that stuck:

"We trained every rep on the sequence. Three months later, I audited the data. Seven of them were still sending from personal Gmail."

Not bad reps. Just too much friction in the system.

We helped her consolidate everything into one platform. Onboarding went from 4 days to 1. Sequence compliance went from 58% to 89% in 6 weeks — right in the middle of a big CRO push, actually.

Is compliance something you're tracking right now?

Saket (Zak) Dalal

one ask

Hi Camilla,

Last one.

Building a sales academy is the right call. The part nobody talks about is the infrastructure underneath it — the tool stack that either makes the playbook easy to follow, or quietly kills adoption before the CRO sees results.

If that's worth a look, I'm happy to spend 20 minutes with you. I'll show you what we're seeing in accounts scaling at a similar pace — you tell me if any of it maps.

No pitch deck. Just a useful 20 minutes.

your reaction

Hi Gourav,

You liked Tal's post last week about why most sales tools create more work than they save.

Made me curious — at Greenly, post-Series B, you're probably right in the middle of exactly that problem. Pipeline expectations have jumped. Headcount is growing. And everyone wants results yesterday, but the systems to support it aren't fully built yet.

Is that a fair read? Or has most of the focus gone into inbound and partnerships so far?

Email · Follow-Up
Day 5 · New angle · The Series B pattern
✉️ Email
ZD
Zak Dalal
zak@lemlist.com
To: Gourav Pathak <gourav@greenly.earth>
the Series B moment

Hi Gourav,

The post-Series B moment is one I see a lot.

Funding lands, headcount doubles, leadership wants a pipeline that matches the new valuation. Suddenly you're onboarding 5-8 reps who all need to be productive immediately.

What usually happens: the stack fragments. Some reps use Sales Nav. Some use Apollo. Some run sequences from their last job. Your data becomes unreliable because it's coming from four different places.

A Sales & Partnerships lead at a climate tech company went through the same thing after their Series B. Consolidated everything into one platform, rebuilt sequences in two weeks. Reply rate went from 8% to 21%. Pipeline doubled in a quarter without adding headcount.

How are you approaching the system side of scaling right now?

Saket (Zak) Dalal
Key Account Manager

system first

Hi Gourav,

Last one.

The teams that come out of a funding round stronger aren't the ones that move fastest. They're the ones that build the system first — then move fast.

If the outbound infrastructure is on your radar — how to get new reps productive quickly, how to make sequences consistent at scale, how to actually measure what's working — I'd love to show you what that looks like in 20 minutes.

📅 Book 20 minutes

To: Francesca Torterola <francesca@eims.com>
after the webinar

Hi Francesca,

You were on Tal's session last week.

Something he said that I imagine hits differently when you're managing SDR teams across multiple clients:

"The sequence doesn't fail because the rep is bad. It fails because the structure doesn't give them a chance to succeed."

With 5–10 SDRs spread across different accounts — how consistent is your sequence structure right now? Same rules across every client, or does it fragment as soon as the brief changes?

Email · Follow-Up
Day 5 · New angle · Multi-client pain
✉️ Email
ZD
Zak Dalal
zak@lemlist.com
To: Francesca Torterola <francesca@eims.com>
the multi-client problem

Hi Francesca,

Here's what I keep hearing from SalesOps managers at agencies: the single-client playbook is hard enough. Across 4–5 accounts with different ICPs, different messaging, different reporting — the real challenge isn't strategy. It's consistency.

One sequence manager we worked with ran 8 SDRs across 6 clients. Every client had a different tool preference. Data lived everywhere. She spent more time reconciling reports than coaching her team.

She consolidated into one platform with unified reporting. Onboarding a new SDR for a new client dropped from a week to two days. Her clients got better results without her team working more hours.

Is reporting clean for you right now, or is it a constant rebuild every time a new client comes on?

Saket (Zak) Dalal
Key Account Manager
+44 7984 125410
saket@lempire.co

one platform

Hi Francesca,

Last one.

What Tal showed in the webinar is genuinely achievable. The part that makes it hard at your scale — across multiple clients — is when the infrastructure underneath isn't built to support it.

One platform. One data source. One place to measure performance across every campaign, every SDR, every client.

If that's worth 20 minutes, I'll show you exactly how it works in practice.
